LAW AND MOTION 302, CROSS DEFENDANT SHENG LIU (SUED AS ROE NO. 1)'S SPECIAL MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT (CCP 425.16). NON-PARTY SHENG LIU'S ANTI-SLAPP MOTION TO STRIKE IS DENIED. SHENG LIU FILED THIS MOTION CLAIMING THAT HE WAS SUED AS ROE 1. THE RECORD DOES NOT SHOW, HOWEVER, THAT BING WU EVER SUBSTITUTED SHENG LIU INTO THIS CASE AS A CROSS-DEFENDANT. AN ANTI-SLAPP MOTION MAY ONLY BE BROUGHT BY A "PARTY" AGAINST WHOM A CAUSE OF ACTION HAS BEEN ASSERTED. (CODE CIV. PROC. 425.16(B)(1), (J)(1); FOUNDATION FOR TAXPAYER & CONSUMER RIGHTS V. GARAMENDI (2005) 132 CAL.APP.4TH 1375, 1392 [INSURER THAT OBTAINED LEAVE TO INTERVENE IN ACTION "DID NOT BECOME A PERSON AGAINST WHOM A CAUSE OF ACTION WAS ASSERTED SIMPLY BY FILING A COMPLAINT IN INTERVENTION AGAINST PETITIONERS. [INSURER] THUS COULD NOT INTERVENE TO MAKE ITSELF A SELF-STYLED 'DEFENDANT' FOR PURPOSES OF AN ANTI-SLAPP MOTION WHEN IT WAS NOT A DEFENDANT TO THE COMPLAINT AND THE COMPLAINT DID NOT PURPORT TO ASSERT ANY CLAIM AGAINST IT."].) SHENG LIU HAS NO STANDING TO FILE A MOTION BECAUSE HE IS NOT A PARTY. "IT IS WELL SETTLED THAT ONLY PARTIES WITH A REAL INTEREST IN A DISPUTE HAVE STANDING TO SEEK ITS ADJUDICATION. (CODE CIV. PROC., 367.)" (KILLIAN V. MILLARD (1991) 228 CAL.APP.3D 1601, 1605.) INDEED, EVEN IF THE COURT AGREED WITH SHENG LIU'S ARGUMENTS, IT COULD NOT GRANT ANY RELIEF OR STRIKE ANY CLAIM BECAUSE NO SUCH CLAIM IS ALLEGED AGAINST HIM.(SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR FULL DETAILS)ORDER IS SIGNED. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Portions Of Bing Wu And Kai Ming Leis Second Amended Cross Complaint
Off Calendar Aug-14-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Brendan J. Dooley, Esq. of Horner Law Group, T:925.943.6570, for cross defendant & moving party;
Steven Yuen, Esq. of Heath & Yuen, T:415.373.3957, for cross complainant & opposing party.

RULING: The matter was argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on CROSS DEFENDANT LIAN TONG, LLC's Motion To Strike Portions Of Bing Wu And Kai Ming Lei's Second Amended Cross Complaint as follows:

Lian Tong LLC's motion to strike is denied. The court exercises its discretion and will hear the motion even though it was filed late.

The court denies Lian Tong's request to strike the claims for $400,000, $61,600, and $20,500. The claimed damages relate to the misrepresentation claims and a factual issue remains regarding what can be recovered. (See Civ. Code Sec. 3333 ["For the breach of an obligation not arising from contract, the measure of damages, except where otherwise expressly provided by this Code, is the amount which will compensate for all the detriment proximately caused thereby, whether it could have been anticipated or not."].)

The court denies the request to strike paragraphs 110 and 112 of the Second Amended Cross-Complaint. As explained in the companion motion, the claim for rescission is well-pleaded and the court's prior order contained a typographical error.

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

RULING: The matter was argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on PLAINTIFF LIAN TONG, LLC'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings as follows:

Cross-defendant Lian Tong, LLC's motion for judgment on the pleadings is denied.

The motion is premature because no answer is on file, but the court will (again) exercise its discretion to hear it. (See Code Civ. Proc. Secs. 438(f)(2), 438(a)(2).)

Lian Tong fails to show that Bing Wu's claims are time-barred. Lian Tong's original complaint was filed on 2/26/16 and alleges that Bing Wu breached the 2014 construction contract. Because the claims in the Second Amended Cross Complaint also arise out of the 2014 construction contract, they are compulsory and relate back to the filing of the original complaint. (See Sidney v. Superior Court (1988) 198 Cal.App.3d 710, 714; Boyer v. Jensen (2005) 129 Cal.App.4th 62, 69-70 ["[T]he filing of his complaint caused the statute of limitations to be tolled or suspended as to causes of action arising out of the same set of facts alleged in the complaint. [Citation.] The principle underlying the rule is that the plaintiff has [by filing the complaint] thereby waived the [statute of limitations] claim and permitted the defendant to make all proper defenses to the cause of action pleaded." (internal quotations omitted)].) Therefore, Lian Tong's discussion regarding when the original or amended cross-complaints were filed is irrelevant.

The court rejects Lian Tong's argument that Bing Wu has "admitted" that the 2015 cancellation agreement did not "rescind" the 2014 construction contract. The court further rejects Lian Tong's litigation privilege argument. The court previously rejected these assertions. (See 5/14/19 Order, 3:1-4; 3:21-4:3.) Moreover, those arguments improperly seek to dispose of only part of a cause of action.

The court denies judgment on the pleadings on the rescission cause of action. Even if Bing Wu cannot recover attorney's fees as damages on the 2015 cancellation agreement, it can sue for rescission of the 2014 construction agreement based on the terms of the 2015 agreement. The court's 5/14/19 order meant to allow Bing Wu to plead a claim for rescission of the 2014 agreement. Its language referring to rescinding the 2015 cancellation agreement was a typographical error.

Order signed in open court.

Defendants Bing Wu, Kai Lei'S Motion For Attorneys Fees Against Lian Tong, Llc
Off Calendar Jun-24-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

Defendants and Cross-Complainants Bing Wu and Kai Ming Lei f/d/b/a Bing Wu Construction Company's motion for attorneys' fees against Plaintiff and Cross-Defendant Lian Tong, LLC is denied. The motion is brought under Code of Civil Procedure Section 425.16(c)(1), which provides that if the court finds that a special motion to strike is "frivolous or is solely intended to cause unnecessary delay, the court shall award costs and reasonable attorney's fees to a plaintiff [here, a cross-complainant] prevailing on the motion, pursuant to Section 128.5."

Defendants' motion is timely. (Carpenter v. Jack in the Box Corp. (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 454, 462-468 [holding that a motion for attorney fees by the prevailing plaintiff on a special motion to strike is timely under Rule 3.1702 of the California Rules of Court if filed before entry of final judgment at the conclusion of the lawsuit]. However, as Plaintiff correctly observes, in opposing its special motion to strike the first amended cross-complaint, Defendants specifically requested the Court to find that the motion was frivolous or was solely intended to cause unnecessary delay. In its March 18, 2019 order denying Plaintiff's motion, the Court made no such finding. Accordingly, the instant motion amounts to an untimely and improper motion for reconsideration. (Code Civ. Proc. 1008(a).)

The motion also fails on the merits. The Court denied Plaintiff's motion on the ground that it was untimely. As Defendants properly recognize, however, "the fact that a special motion to strike was filed untimely, standing alone, cannot support a finding that the motion is frivolous or solely intended to cause unnecessary delay." (Chitsazzadeh v. Kramer & Kaslow (2011) 199 Cal.App.4th 676, 684.) Rather, "whether the sole purpose of the motion is to harass an opposing party or whether the motion is solely intended to cause unnecessary delay also depends on either the objective merits of the motion or some other indication of the moving defendant's subjective motivation and cannot be inferred from the fact that the motion was untimely." (Id.) Here, Defendants do not claim that Plaintiff's subjective motivation in bringing the motion can be inferred from the objective merits (or lack thereof) of the motion. Nor could they because, as Plaintiff points out, on May 14, 2019, this Court granted Plaintiff's motion for judgment on the pleadings as to all six causes of action of the first amended cross-complaint, two of them without leave to amend. Defendants argue that Plaintiffs' subjective motion to cause unnecessary delay can be inferred from Plaintiff's having sought and obtained a continuance of the trial date on the basis of the pendency of its special motion to strike. However, Defendants have not shown that was the only basis for Plaintiff's request for a continuance, which was also based on the status of the pleadings and of discovery, and therefore have not shown that the anti-SLAPP motion was "solely intended to cause unnecessary delay." Accordingly, the motion is denied.

Written order is signed and filed.

Notice Of Motion And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Of Special Motion To Strike Complaint
Off Calendar Mar-18-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Brendan J. Dooley, esq. (925-943-6570) of Horner Law Group PC for Plaintiff.
Steven W. Yuen, esq. (415-622-7064) of Heath Yuen for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PLAINTIFF LIAN TONG, LLC's Special Motion To Strike Complaint (CCP 425.16).

Cross-defendant Lian Tong, LLC's Anti-SLAPP Motion to Strike the First Amended Cross-Complaint is denied as untimely. Code of Civil Procedure 425.16(f) provides that "[t]he special motion may be filed within 60 days of the service of the complaint'." The California Supreme Court has interpreted this provision to mean that "a defendant must move to strike a cause of action within 60 days of service of the earliest complaint that contains that cause of action." (Newport Harbor Ventures, LLC v. Morris Cerullo World Evangelism (2018) 4 Cal.5th 637, 640.)

In this case, Bing Wu's original cross-complaint was filed on September 8, 2016. The gravamen of the six count Cross-Complaint is that the parties entered into a 2014 construction contract, the parties subsequently entered into a 2015 release/cancellation agreement that cancelled the 2014 construction agreement, and cross-defendants have breached the 2015 cancellation agreement and/or acted fraudulently by filing the instant action seeking to recover under the 2014 construction contract. The Cross-Complaint alleges: "[a]t all times, cross-defendants never intended to release and or cancel the March 30, 2014 written contract entered into with cross-complainants, and fraudulently induced cross-complainants into entering the February 5, 2015 written contract, to deprive them of $20,500." (Cross-Complaint 14.) The Cross-Complaint further alleges that: "[a]ll cross-defendants breached the contract by not releasing, and not cancelling the March 30, 2014 written contract entered into with cross-complainants since cross-defendants have sued cross-complainants for breach of the March 30, 2014 written contract." (Cross-Complaint 23 (emphasis added).)

Bing Wu's amended cross-complaint was filed on January 30, 2019. The First Amended Cross-Complaint alleges the same six causes of action and similarly seeks to recover because cross-defendants failed to release or cancel the March 30, 2014 contract "which is partly exemplified by cross-defendants suing cross-complainants for breach of the March 30, 2014 written contract." (First Amended Cross-Complaint, 32.)

The above-cited allegations show that Bing Wu's consistent theory of recovery is that Lian Tong, LLC breached the 2015 release agreement by filing a complaint that seeks to recover on the 2014 construction agreement. The new allegations in the First Amended Cross-Complaint that amplify the allegations regarding Lian Tong, LLC's alleged misrepresentations did not change the nature of the underlying causes of action. Lian Tong, LLC could have attacked the Cross-Complaint based on the petitioning allegations in paragraph 23, but chose not to. Instead, the parties litigated the matter and engaged in extensive discovery over the past two years. Allowing the anti-SLAPP motion at this point would be antithetical to the purpose of the anti-SLAPP statute, which is to quickly and expeditiously dispose of meritless suits. (Newport Harbor Ventures, LLC, 4 Cal.5th at 645.) Therefore, the motion is denied. (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript).

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

Notice Of Motion And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Of Special Motion To Strike Complaint
Off Calendar Mar-18-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Brendan J. Dooley, esq. (925-943-6570) of Horner Law Group PC for Plaintiff.
Steven W. Yuen, esq. (415-622-7064) of Heath Yuen for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT SHENG Liu (sued as Roe No. 1)'s Special Motion To Strike Complaint (CCP 425.16).

Non-party Sheng Liu's Anti-SLAPP Motion to Strike is denied. Sheng Liu filed this motion claiming that he was sued as Roe 1. The record does not show, however, that Bing Wu ever substituted Sheng Liu into this case as a cross-defendant. An anti-SLAPP motion may only be brought by a "party" against whom a cause of action has been asserted. (Code Civ. Proc. 425.16(b)(1), (j)(1); Foundation for Taxpayer & Consumer Rights v. Garamendi (2005) 132 Cal.App.4th 1375, 1392 [insurer that obtained leave to intervene in action "did not become a person against whom a cause of action was asserted simply by filing a complaint in intervention against petitioners. [Insurer] thus could not intervene to make itself a self-styled 'defendant' for purposes of an anti-SLAPP motion when it was not a defendant to the complaint and the complaint did not purport to assert any claim against it."].) Sheng Liu has no standing to file a motion because he is not a party. "It is well settled that only parties with a real interest in a dispute have standing to seek its adjudication. (Code Civ. Proc., 367.)" (Killian v. Millard (1991) 228 Cal.App.3d 1601, 1605.) Indeed, even if the Court agreed with Sheng Liu's arguments, it could not grant any relief or strike any claim because no such claim is alleged against him.

The alter ego allegations in the First Amended Cross-Complaint did not add Sheng Liu as a party. Sheng Liu's authorities that provide that an alter ego defendant-i.e. a party-can challenge a pleading or charge of alter ego liability do not give a non-party standing to file a motion. "The alter ego doctrine arises when a plaintiff comes into court claiming that an opposing party is using the corporate form unjustly and in derogation of the plaintiff's interests." (Mesler v. Bragg Management Co. (1985) 39 Cal.3d 290, 300 (emphasis added).)

Sheng Liu has no due process right to appear at this point. If Bing Wu ultimately seeks to hold Sheng Liu liable as an alter ego judgment debtor, Bing Wu will need to file a motion and provide Sheng Liu notice. (See NEC Electronics Inc. v. Hurt (1989) 208 Cal.App.3d 772, 778-780.) Moreover, it would only be proper to add Sheng Liu as a debtor if he was in fact controlling this litigation through Lian Tong, LLC. Therefore, Sheng Liu's contention that he needs to be part of this case now to protect his rights rings hollow. (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript).

Order is signed.

Defendant'S Motion To Compel The Deposition Of Lian Tong, Llc'S Owner And Agent Yi Fang Liu And Request For Monetary Sanctions
Off Calendar May-10-2017 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance on this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling.
Defendants' motion to compel the deposition of Yi Fan Liu is denied and all requests for sanctions are denied. Defendants have not shown that Yi Fang Liu is an officer, director, managing agent or employee of plaintiff Lian Tong, LLC and thus his deposition cannot be required without a subpoena. However, to avoid gamesmanship and unfairness to defendants, plaintiff may not call Yi Fang Liu as a witness at trial or use any declaration from him on any motion unless and until it makes arrangements for defendants to depose Yi Fang Liu, even if that deposition must occur by videoconference in the event that Yi Fang Liu is in China and defendants' counsel is unable or unwilling to go to China. Since defendants' positions on this motion did not lack substantial justification, no sanctions are awarded against defendants. Order signed. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Carol Karen CSR #8189 =302/hk
